questions.q: What does the I-F-N mnemonic stand for as the syllabus' three communication issues?
options: Involve and empower, enable Feedback, Negotiate
Identify, Formalise, Notify
Inform, Facilitate, Notify
Interview, Feedback, Negotiation
questions.explanation: I-F-N stands for Involve and empower the client, enable Feedback, and Negotiate — the three communication issues named in the syllabus. The script presents I-F-N as your structure for any 'how do you communicate with the client' question, including the six-marker. The other options use plausible-sounding I-F-N words like 'Identify' and 'Inform' but are not the syllabus terms.

questions.q: Identify the four ethical areas captured by the PABT ("pab-it") mnemonic.
options: Privacy, Accountability, Bias, Trust
Performance, Accessibility, Budget, Transparency
Privacy, Accessibility, Bias, Transparency
Protection, Accuracy, Behaviour, Testing
questions.explanation: PABT stands for Privacy, Accessibility, Bias, and Transparency — the four ethical issues in project work, each a callback to another module. Option A is tempting because 'Accountability' and 'Trust' sound ethical, but the syllabus terms the script uses are Accessibility and Transparency. PABT is your checklist of ethical issues for any ethics question.

questions.q: Which syllabus outcome does this episode address?
options: Designs, produces and evaluates appropriate solutions to a range of problems
Applies structured processes to manage software projects and schedules
Justifies the selection and use of tools and resources to develop creative solutions
Explains the social, ethical and legal implications of software engineering on the individual, society and the environment
questions.explanation: The script names the outcome SE-12-05: explains the social, ethical and legal implications of software engineering on the individual, society and the environment. The episode stresses there is no algorithm here — marks come from naming the issues precisely and giving structured answers about working with people, which is exactly what this outcome targets.

questions.q: Explain what "empower" specifically means when involving a client, beyond simply involving them.
options: Giving the client full administrative access to the development environment
Sending the client frequent status reports so they are always informed of progress
Giving the client genuine influence over the outcome, so their input actually changes the product rather than just being informed after the fact
Allowing the client to approve the final product before it is released to users
questions.explanation: The script defines empowerment as 'involvement with teeth' — giving the client genuine influence over the outcome so their feedback actually changes the product, not just informing them after a decision is made. Option B describes mere involvement (keeping them informed), which is the weaker idea empowerment goes beyond. Co-design activities, where the client helps make design decisions, are the mechanism.

questions.q: Outline how a developer enables effective feedback during a project.
options: By asking the client what they think at the final demonstration before release
By collecting as much feedback as possible in one comprehensive end-of-project survey
By providing multiple channels and regular cycles, categorising input, and tracking how each piece of feedback changed decisions
By recording all client comments in a document for reference after the project ends
questions.explanation: Enabling feedback is a structured, two-way process: multiple channels (surveys, interviews, usability sessions), regular cycles aligned to iterations, categorisation by type and priority, and tracking how feedback changed decisions to close the loop. The single end-of-project survey is the exact trap the script warns against — one overwhelming dump rather than steady cycles. The forward hook for analysing feedback is C-S-A (Collect, Synthesise, Act) in 26-02.

questions.q: List, in order, the four steps of the structured scope-negotiation process.
options: Refuse the request, document the refusal, inform the client, escalate if needed
Clarify the underlying need, assess impact and feasibility, explore options, document the agreement
Estimate cost, present a quote, get sign-off, schedule the work
Gather requirements, prioritise features, build a prototype, release
questions.explanation: Negotiation follows four steps: clarify the underlying need (the literal request isn't always the best fix), assess impact and feasibility, explore options (full, simplified, alternative, or defer), and document what was agreed and why. Option A is wrong because negotiation is neither a flat 'no' nor a blanket 'yes' — it is clarify, assess, offer options, document, conducted around the Scope-Time-Cost triangle.

questions.q: Distinguish a weak approach to involving a client from a strong one, as the script frames it.
options: A weak approach uses email while a strong approach uses video calls and in-person meetings
A weak approach involves only the client while a strong approach surveys every end user as well
A weak approach skips documentation while a strong approach documents every conversation in detail
A weak approach gathers requirements once at the start and delivers at the end, while a strong approach builds an ongoing partnership with regular demonstrations and co-design
questions.explanation: The script contrasts the weak developer — gathers requirements in week one, disappears for six months, delivers at the end — with the strong developer who builds an ongoing partnership where the client feels heard, informed and in control through regular demonstrations and co-design. The distinguishing factor is continuous involvement versus a one-off, not the communication medium or documentation habits, which are the tempting but incorrect options.

questions.q: Why is treating communication and ethics as "soft" or optional described as the most costly trap on this dot-point?
options: Because projects fail far more often from poor communication and ignored stakeholders than from technical impossibility — a project that ignores feedback builds the wrong thing efficiently
Because markers deduct marks automatically for any answer that mentions code
Because ethical and legal regulations legally require communication plans in all software projects
Because communication tasks take more time than coding tasks in most project schedules
questions.explanation: The script argues the 'real work is the code' mindset is wrong: history shows projects fail more from poor communication and ignored stakeholders than from technical limits — a project that ignores feedback 'builds the wrong thing efficiently.' Healthcare.gov is cited as failing partly because stakeholder communication broke down. The 'legally required' option invents a mandate the script never claims; the real point is that communication is core engineering discipline, not a nice-to-have.

questions.q: A school proposes a student performance analytics system that flags "at-risk" students using academic, attendance and behavioural data. Using PABT, which combination of ethical tensions does this scenario raise?
options: Only privacy, because the data is collected on minors
Privacy (sensitive data on minors), Bias (biased history producing unfair flags), and Transparency (opaque labelling with a self-fulfilling-prophecy risk), alongside competing stakeholder interests
Only accessibility, because some students may not be able to read the flags
Primarily transparency, since the algorithm's accuracy is the only concern
questions.explanation: The anchor scenario deliberately collides across multiple PABT issues: Privacy (sensitive behavioural data on minors), Bias (a model trained on biased history could unfairly flag groups — 'the algorithm isn't neutral'), and Transparency (an opaque 'at-risk' label with self-fulfilling-prophecy risk), plus competing stakeholder interests (admin wants data, teachers fear bias, parents want help but also privacy, students fear labelling). Options A and C are wrong because they pick a single issue when the scenario's value is that it touches several at once.

questions.q: A client keeps requesting new features mid-project but refuses to extend the deadline or budget. Recommend the most appropriate response and justify it.
options: Refuse all new requests outright to protect the agreed scope and avoid scope creep
Silently accept the requests to keep the client happy and absorb the extra work
Negotiate around the Scope-Time-Cost triangle: clarify the underlying need, assess impact, explore options such as a simplified version, swap, or defer, and document the agreement
Extend the team size so all new features can be delivered within the original deadline
questions.explanation: The correct response is to negotiate, not blanket-refuse or silently accept: clarify the underlying need behind each request, assess impact and feasibility, explain that fixing time and cost while raising scope means something must give, then offer options (simplify, swap a lower-priority feature, or defer to a later phase) and document what is agreed. Option A is a flat 'no' and option B a blanket 'yes' — both rejected by the script. This protects project boundaries while keeping the client in control of priorities, framing the conversation as a shared trade-off.
